problem_summary: 2013 MCM Problem B (discrete) — "The Water Problem": design a 13-year (2013–2025) water strategy for a chosen water-scarce country (this team chose China). The team decomposed the open-ended policy problem into five linked sub-models: (1) forecast national water demand/supply, (2) store water to fix temporal imbalance + transfer water to fix spatial imbalance, (3) desalinate seawater for coastal cities, (4) conserve water via pollution control + water-saving, and (5) evaluate/compare the four strategies to recommend an optimal combination. Final deliverable includes a position paper to China's leadership.
models_used: Function-fit forecasting: linear / power / interpolant (shape-preserving spline) regression of total water use vs. year, compared by SSE / R-square / adjusted R-square / RMSE (linear R^2=0.776, power R^2=0.783)

Grey system forecasting: Verhulst grey model (chosen over GM(1,1) because the water series is non-monotonic/oscillatory) with accumulated generating operation (AGO), least-squares parameter estimation a,b = (B^T B)^{-1} B^T Y, whitening ODE dX^(1)/dt + aX^(1) = b; forecasts 2025 demand = 6770.1 hundred-million m^3

Grey relational (correlation degree) analysis to rank drivers of each water-use component (agriculture corr 0.9998 to food production/population; industry corr 0.9997 to industrial GDP; live-use to population)
Coefficient of Variation (CV = sigma/mu) of provincial surface water as a reservoir-need index; cost-benefit ratio eta = C/E with E = E0*omega*log(C), C = k*Delta_O, to rank reservoir projects

Water-transfer optimization as a graph problem: provinces = nodes with attributes (Delta_w, watershed b, terrace e, lat/long); great-circle distance d_ij = r*arccos(...); benefit eta_ij with constraints (transfer only high→low terrace b_i<=b_j, only across watersheds e_i!=e_j, deficit→surplus); edge weight omega = 1/eta; Minimum Spanning Tree to extract the 6 optimal national transfer routes

Desalination timing optimization: cost decays p = p0*e^{-sigma*t}; minimize total two-plant cost (1/2)p0*theta(t + T*e^{-sigma*t}) over build time, closed-form optimum t_opt = ln(T*sigma)/sigma (~5 years)

Pollution-control prioritization: watershed demand gamma_k = alpha_k * e^{-rho_k}; city COD accumulation with upstream decay C_kj = sum cod_kj * e^{-(j-i)}; minimize total urgency mu_k by gradient descent (optimal COD cut = -grad mu_k)
Water-saving index beta = D/GDP (water per unit GDP) benchmarked against national average beta0=0.0255 to flag low-efficiency provinces
AHP (Analytic Hierarchy Process): pairwise judgment matrices for economic/physical/environmental impacts across the 4 strategies, with CI/RI consistency check (CR<0.1, RI=0.508 for n=3)

Neural-network strategy evaluation: feed AHP-derived attribute vector m_i=(ec_i, ph_i, en_i, others_i) of real projects (Three Gorges, South-North Transfer, Tianjin Dagang desalination, Taihu sewage) labeled by quality grade de_i (1–10) to classify strategy quality and remove subjectivity
key_techniques: Five-model decomposition that maps one-to-one onto the four physical levers of water supply (store / move / create / conserve) plus a meta-evaluator — turning an open policy prompt into solvable sub-problems

Deliberate model-upgrade narrative: start with naive function-fit, expose its weakness (only 14 yrs of data, low confidence), then justify the Verhulst grey model as the fix — shows scientific self-criticism

Cross-validation of forecasts against authoritative external sources (State Council 7000 hundred-million m^3 cap; 'China sustainable water development' study 7000–8000) to argue credibility rather than just internal residual checks
Reformulating an infrastructure-planning problem as a constrained graph/MST problem with physically meaningful constraints (terrace gravity flow, cross-watershed rule)
Real-world validation hook: MST output routes (Lines 4/5/6) coincide with the actual Middle/West/East routes of China's South-North Water Diversion Project — strong evidence the model is right
Closed-form analytic optimum for desalination build timing (t_opt = ln(T*sigma)/sigma) instead of pure numerics
Reusing one core machinery (water supply pressure alpha, benefit ratio eta = benefit/cost) consistently across all four strategy models so Model 5 can compare them on a common footing

Hybrid AHP→Neural-Network pipeline: AHP supplies interpretable attribute weights, NN learns the mapping from attributes to a quality grade using real labeled projects, explicitly to 'eliminate subjective factors' of pure AHP
Heavy use of real provincial/watershed data from China Statistical Yearbook, with MATLAB code in the appendix (regression, GM(1,1), grey relational analysis) for reproducibility
why_it_won: "From a judge's view, this paper wins on completeness, realism, and a coherent decision-narrative rather than on any single sophisticated algorithm. It fully answers an open, multi-part policy prompt with a five-model system in which every sub-model feeds the next (forecast → storage/transfer → desalination → conservation → comparative evaluation → position paper), so the work reads as one integrated strategy, not five disconnected exercises. It validates results against external authority (matches the State Council cap and reproduces the real South-North Water Diversion routes via MST), which is the single most convincing thing a judge can see — the model independently rediscovers reality. It shows mathematical maturity by self-diagnosing the weak function-fit model and upgrading to a Verhulst grey model with stated justification, and it spans a wide, well-chosen toolbox (grey forecasting, CV, MST, constrained optimization, closed-form calculus, gradient descent, AHP, neural net) each applied to a fitting sub-problem. The strong summary sheet, consistent nomenclature, cost-benefit ratio reused everywhere, and a closing position paper aimed at the actual stakeholder all signal the executive-communication and modeling judgment that O-award judges reward."
section_highlights: "Summary sheet (lines 31–39) is dense and concrete: it names every model, the actual method used, AND headline numbers (2025 demand 6770; transfer Yangtze→Yellow/Hai) — a judge can grasp the whole paper in 30 seconds. Nomenclature table (line 145) defines ~45 symbols up front, keeping the long paper readable. The forecasting section (3.3→3.4) is a model of honest iteration: it builds function-fit, tabulates SSE/R^2/RMSE, admits low confidence, then switches to Verhulst grey with full derivation and a 14-year residual/relative-error validation table. The water-transfer section (4.3) is the technical centerpiece: clean attribute encoding P_i(Delta_w, b, e, x, y), great-circle distance matrix, an explicit constrained objective z=min{eta} with s.t. block, a worked Beijing example, then the MST national result validated against the real South-North project. The closing position paper (Section 9) translates math into stakeholder-facing prose. Appendix ships runnable MATLAB for regression, GM(1,1), and grey relational analysis."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Model 5 (the headline 'optimal strategy' selector) is only specified, not executed — the neural network is described conceptually with a tiny 4-row illustrative training table and no trained weights, outputs, or actual ranking of the strategies; the promised 'optimal combination' is never quantitatively delivered

Many cost/benefit formulas use undetermined proportionality constants (E0, k, k0, K0, omega) that are never estimated from data, so the eta values are relative/ordinal only — the paper hand-waves with 'determine parameters using Data Mining' without doing it

Forecasting rests on only ~14 years (1997–2010) of data, which the team itself flags; the Verhulst/GM(1,1) terminology is used somewhat interchangeably and the parameter sigma=5 in desalination and sigma=5 build-time are asserted as 'reasonable' without sensitivity analysis

Numerous OCR/transcription artifacts and unresolved symbols ('??', mismatched subscripts like cod_kj vs e^{-(j-i)} vs e^{-(i-j)}) suggest the derivations were not always internally consistent or cleanly typeset

Pollution model assumes annual emissions never exceed self-recovery (no cumulative effect), then contradicts itself by later adding a carry-over term phi*C(Y-1) only in a side 'feasibility' note — the main optimization ignores it

Groundwater is dropped entirely (W ~ U+O then 'neglect U'), and several strong simplifications (max 2 desalination plants/city, linear coastal demand growth, no climate change) limit realism of a 13-year forecast
English prose is rough in places (grammar, 'finger out', religious flourish in the intro) — tolerated by judges but not exemplary
reusable_patterns: Decompose an open policy/strategy prompt into a chain of sub-models where each output feeds the next, then add a final meta-model that compares the sub-strategies on a common metric (here eta = benefit/cost) — a clean template for any multi-part MCM/ICM problem

The 'forecast-then-upgrade' move: present a simple baseline (regression/function-fit), quantify its weakness with goodness-of-fit stats, then justify a more advanced method (grey model) — demonstrates judgment and earns credibility

Validate model output against a real-world ground truth (official targets, existing infrastructure) — reproducing the actual South-North Water Diversion routes via MST is the gold-standard sanity check judges love

Encode geographic/discrete planning problems as attributed graphs (nodes with physical attributes, great-circle distance edges, benefit-based weights) and solve with MST / constrained optimization — directly transferable to logistics, network, and routing problems

Define a normalized stress/benchmark index (water supply pressure alpha vs national average alpha0; water-per-GDP beta vs beta0) to rank and triage regions — a reusable pattern for prioritization across any spatial dataset

Pair AHP (interpretable weights + CR consistency check) with a learning model (NN classifier on real labeled cases) to combine expert judgment with data and defend against the 'AHP is subjective' critique
Get closed-form optima where possible (t_opt = ln(T*sigma)/sigma from minimizing a cost function) instead of only numerical search — shows analytical strength cheaply

Write a stakeholder-facing position paper / letter at the end that translates results into a concrete recommendation — satisfies the 'communicate to a decision-maker' requirement common in ICM/policy problems
Ship runnable solver code (MATLAB) in the appendix for the core numerical methods to signal reproducibility
